title: Full Episode: TODAY Show - March 3
author: NBC News
fullText: Good Tuesday morning. War spreads across the Middle East >> with a new wave of attacks and a warning to Americans abroad. It's March 3rd. This is Today escalation. The US military delivering punishing strike after strike inside Iran, targeting its navy and infrastructure. >> Cover the windows. Iran retaliating with their own attacks across the region. US embassies evacuated. The global oil supply already suffering with prices at the pump set to skyrocket. As the White House maintains the assault is just starting. We're live in the region. Behind closed doors, newly released video showing former President Bill Clinton answering questions about his relationship with Jeffrey Epstein. Have you ever engaged in sexual activity with a person introduced to you by Jeffrey Epstein? >> No. >> His wife, former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton, nearly walking out in frustration. >> If you guys are doing that, I am done. You can hold me in contempt from now until the cows come home. >> We're live with what their hours of testimony reveal and what happens next. Emotional moment. Savannah and her family return to their mother NY's home, delivering a message of hope and love. As the investigation enters its second month, the sheriff sharing new details about the case, the evidence, and the hunt for a suspect. Winter's last gasp. Tens of millions waking up to bitter cold snow and ice again. But major changes and a taste of spring just around the corner. Al's tracking all of it. Those stories. Plus, facing the music. Entertainment giants Live Nation and Ticket Master accused of running a ticket monopoly and driving up costs for some of the biggest acts. >> Why is one ticket starting to cost $1,000 before tax? >> The landmark trial that could shake up the industry and finally bring prices down for fans. >> And have you heard? You're going to see things that you've never seen before here at Elephant Valley. >> An exclusive tour of a massive new zoo exhibit years in the making where you can get closer than ever to some gentle giants. We'll take you there today, Tuesday, March 3rd, 2026. From NBC News, this is Today with Savannah Guthrie and Greg Melvin. From Studio 1A in Rockefeller Plaza and a very good morning to you. Welcome to Today. Thank you so much for starting your Tuesday morning with us. Chanel is in for Savannah today as she remains with her family. We want to get right to that top story this morning. The war with Iran quickly intensifying overnight as we now enter day four. We want to get you up to speed with the very latest this morning. The Saudi Defense Ministry says the US embassy in Riad was attacked by two drones overnight. A Saudi official telling NBC News no one was injured. >> President Trump promising a swift response to the attack. The president also suggesting the US operation could go far longer than four to 5 weeks. It comes as the State Department is now ordering Americans from 14 countries in the Middle East to depart now due to serious safety risks. >> We've got it all covered for you this morning, including the economic impact of the conflict. But we start once again with NBC's Richard Engel. He is in Israel. Richard, good morning. >> Good morning. I am this morning in the town of Paktikva, which is a suburb just outside of Tel Aviv. And a short while ago, an Iranian missile that was destroyed by Israel's missile defense systems came crashing down here. It smashed into this house, destroying the garage. They are already trying to clean up the debris here. No one was killed in this incident, but Israelis, Americans, and Iranians are being killed in this war, which shows no signs of slowing down. Israel and the US this morning are tearing Iran's military and leadership to pieces with new attacks overnight and this morning. >> Rocking Thran and other cities as the Pentagon raised the death toll to at least six US service members killed by an Iranian attack on a base in Kuwait. President Trump says the war could last four to five weeks or longer. >> They said, "Oh, well, President wants to do it really quickly. After that, he'll get bored. I don't get bored. There's nothing boring about this. >> More American troops and jets are being deployed to join the fight. >> The hardest hits are yet to come. >> Still, huge crowds of Iranians flooded the streets for a mass funeral. Iran says more than 150 people were killed by a US or Israeli attack on a girl school this weekend. The US military says it's investigating what happened. This is now an expanding regional war in Lebanon. This morning, Israel is dramatically intensifying the fight against the pro-Iranian militia Hezbollah. The Israeli military says it's seizing more Lebanese territory after Hezbollah fired rockets into Israel. While Iran is fighting on multiple fronts, the US embassy in Riad was hit by drones and caught fire. The embassy in Aman, Jordan, also evacuated staff. >> The State Department issued a blanket warning advising Americans to evacuate by commercial means from 14 countries covering most of the Middle East. But the thousands of American government workers, visitors, and long-term residents in the region have few options with most airports closed. And we're seeing more of what happened to those American pilots accidentally shot down by Kuwaiti air defense in friendly fire yesterday. The crews ejected and got to safety. Kuwaitis tried to help this American pilot. >> You're fine. >> Really? You need something to help you? >> No, I'm okay. >> No problem. You're unsafe. You're unsafe. You're safe. Red Crescent said that nearly 800 Iranians have been killed since this war began. The group didn't distinguish between soldiers and civilians. Craig, >> chief foreign correspondent Richard Engel force there in Israel. Richard, thank you. >> Meanwhile, top members of the Trump administration expected on Capitol Hill today where more classified briefings on the war are planned, but there is growing opposition among some members of Congress. NBC's senior White House correspondent Gabe Gutierrez has the latest. Gabe, good morning. >> Chanel, good morning. The president and his team are now focused on selling this war. Overnight, the president sending a letter to Congress saying it's not possible at this time to know the full scope and duration of military operations that may be necessary. But Democrats are pushing back. >> As the war in Iran escalates, President Trump says the US is ahead of schedule. >> We projected four to five weeks, but uh we have capability to go far longer than that. The administration said over the weekend that Iran was threatening US forces. Now, Secretary of State Marco Rubio explaining it this way. >> We knew that there was going to be an Israeli action. We knew that that would precipitate an attack against American forces. >> Saying the US had to move fast to avoid higher casualties. >> The imminent threat was that we knew that if Iran was attacked and we believe they would be attacked that they would immediately come after us. We went proactively in a defensive way. >> Democrats aren't buying it. But if we equate a threat to Israel is the equivalent of an imminent threat to the United States, then we are in uncharted territory. >> Overnight, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu speaking on Fox News. >> You're not going to have an endless war. This terror regime in Iran is the weakest point that it's been here. >> Echoing Defense Secretary Pete Haggath, who earlier insisted the war wouldn't be forever. >> This is not Iraq. This is not endless. But there has been mixed messaging. >> The objective was not regime change. The objective was to take out those missiles. >> On Saturday, the president said this to the Iranian people, suggesting regime change. >> When we are finished, take over your government. It will be yours to take. >> Now, he's stressing four objectives for the US mission. destroying Iran's missile capabilities, annihilating their navy, preventing Iran from obtaining a nuclear weapon, and ensuring they can no longer fund proxy terror groups outside the country. >> This was our last best chance to strike. >> Now, one question for a lot of people is whether this conflict could involve American troops on the ground. Yesterday, the president and a defense secretary would not rule out boots on the ground, but President Trump is focusing on the military air strikes for now, saying he wants to hit Iran where it hurts. Chanel. >> All right, Gabe. Thanks. >> All right, let's get some perspective now on the war and where this conflict could be headed next. Retired Colonel Steve Warren is an NBC military analyst, former Pentagon spokesman. Uh, Colonel, good morning to you. Thanks for being here with us this morning. First of all, give us a sense of where the Iranian military was before we started our strikes four days ago and where you suspect they are right now. >> So, the Iranian military has a great reputation as being a strong and powerful military in the Middle East. But what I'll tell you, what many may have forgotten since the 12-day war that Israel launched against the Iranians, they have been steadily being degraded. Okay? Of course, the United States you took part in that war a little bit, but particularly the Israelis have been slowly and steadily degrading them. They have a strong ground force. They have an advanced intermediate range missile force, but a lot of the things in between have really been degraded. >> Uh the president outlined, we just talked about this, a timeline of four to five weeks and didn't rule out boots on the ground. How likely are either of those and how do we know when those objectives are reached? I think think of boots on the ground in two ways. A large invasion force, which is what would be required to invade and take over Iran. I do not believe that will happen. But remember, boots on the ground is a slang term, not a military phrase. So the other way to think about it is special operations forces, which I believe we will probably see at some point. So Americans in Iran, yes, large combat forces with tanks and artillery, I believe. No. >> Let's talk about this next wave. Secretary of State Rubio yesterday alluded to the the next wave um being perhaps far more significant than what we've seen over the last day last few days. What could that look like, Colonel? >> Well, the the Pentagon has laid out some very specific military objectives, including sinking the Navy, destroying their intermediate range, ballistic missile range capabilities, and some and some and some nuclear things. I think this next wave, what we should keep an eye on is for an opportunity for the American military to now officially sink the Iranian Navy or to make a real hard push against their nuclear capability >> because we know where their ships are pretty much. >> Well, the ships are the best, right? Because you can count them. We know how many they have. We know where they are. And one single focused assault on the naval capabilities could be decisive. >> All right. Retired Colonel Steve Warren. Welcome to the team, by the way, Colonel. Thanks for being here. >> Thank you. It's great to be here. >> All right. Meanwhile, the war is already sending oil prices skyrocketing. Iran's military says it has shut down one of the world's busiest shipping channels, and we're going to feel it here at home. NBC News senior business correspondent Christine Romans is here with that part of the story. Good morning. Let's dig in here. Can we expect gas prices to keep rising? >> Yeah, they've already happened here. When you look at AAA gas prices, $310 a gallon. That's the national average. That's up a lot from last week. And it's really been a dramatic and quick move. One analyst telling us this morning you could see $3.20 a gallon by the end of the week. Crude oil up uh you know almost 14% since this crisis began. That's where this all begins. The idea that there's some sort of a a global shock to energy production and energy transit through that straight of Hormuz. The Iranian state media saying that uh Iran will start on fire, light on fire any ship that tries to go through there. So when I look at marine traffic, I can see it's a big parking lot on both sides of the straight of Hormuz as as international shippers try to figure out just how much further this conflict will go and that's what's sending up energy prices and we're expecting a really kind of a tough day for stocks this morning >> when 20% of oil goes through that street. Um the administration, have they talked about a plan to address the rising cost of gas in this country as a result? Yeah, the Secretary of State, Marco Rubio, suggested in a stakeout yesterday there'd be an announcement today from the energy secretary and the Treasury Secretary of a program to address what they had anticipated, which is that energy prices would rise because of this uh these attacks in Iran. What's interesting, it was a week ago today that the president was giving the State of the Union address and he was heralding falling energy prices as as something that was good, a good byproduct of his administration. You now have oil prices that have completely reversed all of the uh progress under the Trump administration are now higher. They're back January 20, 2025 levels for oil prices. We have to see what happens in the straight of Hormuz. The last 12 hours have been um have been dangerous in the region quite frankly and there's been an escalation from the Iranians in Saudi Arabia in Qatar and so you've got these big oil and energy producers shutting down operations. All of that will drive energy prices higher and that will translate into higher gas prices at the pump here. >> All right, Christine, thank you. >> Well, this morning we are getting our first look at close to 9 hours of footage from the closed door depositions of Bill and Hillary Clinton. They each testified before members of Congress as part of the Jeffrey Epstein investigation. NBC's Ryan Nobles has been pouring through that video. He's got some noteworthy moments to look at this morning. Ryan, good morning. >> Hey, Greg. Good morning. And the two Clintons each took questions for more than four and a half hours. And despite being pressed by both Republicans and Democrats, they forcefully denied any knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein's crimes. Former President Bill Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton taking tough questions about their relationships and interaction with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. The former president making it clear he knew nothing of the late sex offender's crimes. Have you ever engaged in sexual activity with a person introduced to you by Jeffrey Epstein? >> No. >> For hours forced to explain the many times he appeared in the Epstein files, including this photo of him in a hot tub with a woman. >> I don't know who that is. >> Did you engage in any sexual activities with this person? >> And while critical of the committee calling for his wife to be deposed, acknowledging they were right to call on him. >> I do think you should be talking to me. I think you should have called me. I did take those plane trips with him. And you have a right to ask those questions. >> Clinton also testifying he only ever had one conversation with President Trump about Epstein. >> The president never, this is 20ome years ago, never said anything to me to make me think he was involved in anything improper with regard to to Epstein either. Meanwhile, the former Secretary of State almost left her deposition after Congresswoman Lauren Boowart shared a photo of her with a conservative influencer. >> I'm done with this. If you guys are doing that, I am done. You can hold me in contempt from now till the cows come home. >> She forcefully denied any relationship with Epstein despite being asked about it over and over. >> I am so tired of answering that question. If you have one sent of evidence to the contrary, put it forward. And while the former president said he had no reason to believe that Trump did anything improper related to Epstein, he also said that Trump told him he had ended his relationship over a ba bad land deal which contradicts Trump's claim that he kicked Epstein out of his Mara Lago club over how he treated women. Craig >> Ryan Nobles from Washington for us there. Ryan, thank you. >> All right, let's say good morning to Al now. He's here with the first check of the weather. Good morning. >> Good morning, guys, and good morning to you and hopefully winter's last gaff. We'll see here in the Northeast at least. We've got this area of rain and frozen preip pushing in 34 million of us from Boston to Beckley, West Virginia with the winter storm warnings there for the mountains. We are going to watch this push through interior sections of the Northeast and New England. See some icy travel, but rain and melting snow from Boston to Philadelphia as temperatures actually do warm up. You will see though anywhere from a hundth of an inch to a tenth of an inch of ice stretching from Bradford, PA down to Hagers Hagerstown. Boston could see some icing to the west and we're looking at anywhere from 3 to six inches of snow making its way into interior sections of New England. Light snow there. Record highs down through Texas, very mild out west and a few showers in the northern Rockies. We're going to get to your local forecast coming up in the next 30 seconds. Messi with such immaculate poise and these scenes will live forever as Lionel Messi and Argentina have won the World Cup. >> Back now at 7:30 as Carson joins us. And we are throwing it back to that unforgettable moment from four years ago because today marks 100 days until the next World Cup with matches taking place all across the United States and Telmundo and Peacock are certainly the Spanish language home for the summer of soccer. >> Well, it's fun that the competition internationally is continuing from the Olympics. We have the World Baseball Classic later this month. We have World Cup to look forward to. >> I was just about to say I think the key is looking forward to it. I think we're all ready for something. producer downstairs, Lucas Vasquez, has been celebrating Argentina's win for the last four years. So, something to look forward to 100 days from now. Uh, we are going to start this half hour though with a search for Nancy Guthrie. Today marking 31 days since Savannah's mom went missing. >> And yesterday, our friend Savannah and her family visited NY's home. They placed flowers and a cart at the growing memorial there. >> Yeah, we're also learning more from the sheriff about where things stand in this investigation. NBC's Liz Court sat down with him. Liz, good morning to you. >> Hi Chanel. Hi guys. Good morning to all of you. The sheriff tells us as they enter this next phase of the investigation, he now has a dedicated team from his homicide division working with the FBI on the search for Nancy. The sheriff says that he is very much still operating under the belief that Nancy is alive and that investigators have thousands of leads. They continue to comb through. An emotional moment outside Nancy Guthri's home Monday. Savannah, Annie, and her husband Tomaso visiting that growing memorial seen in this video laying yellow flowers and a card with this handwritten message. Though we are surrounded by so much darkness and uncertainty, our love burns bright. We love you, Mommy. We miss you so much. Savannah later sharing this image on Instagram saying, "We feel the love and prayers from our neighbors, from the Tucson community, and from around the country." Puma County Sheriff Chris Nanos tells us he's also not giving up hope. So, Sheriff, it's been 30 days since Nancy Guthrie went missing. Are investigators any closer to finding the suspect or suspects? >> I think the investigators are are definitely closer. We got a lot of intel, a lot of leads, but now it's time to just go to work. >> We also asked about that new Ring camera video obtained by Fox News Digital showing a car 2 and a half miles away. >> Is this really something we should be looking at? >> Look, what I would tell you is this. We're aware of it and we're looking into it just like any other piece of evidence. Have you been able to identify the car that drove past at 2:36 a.m.? >> No. >> No. >> But you are looking to identify it. >> We're looking at that vehicle as well as hundreds of thousands of other vehicles that were out driving that time of day. >> Nano says they still haven't identified the other clothes the suspect was wearing and told us this about the backpack. >> We've now learned that maybe it wasn't purchased out of Walmart. That backpack as new is exclusive to Walmart. But who's to say I didn't buy it and put it on eBay? That's what we're looking at. >> With so many questions, he told us there is a lot they're just not sharing. >> There's so much that everybody wants to know, right? But I would be very neglectful, irresponsible as a police law enforcement leader to share that with everybody. We have information on this case that we think is going to hopefully lead us to solving this case, but it takes time >> and you'll get there. Absolutely. Absolutely. >> All right. Well, that's that's good to hear, Liz. And by the way, you asked a lot of questions that have been top of mind for a lot of folks who've been following this case very closely. Um, what what did the sheriff say, if anything, about the DNA that was found at the house? >> Yeah, Craig, that's a big question. I did ask him about that. He said that they are still having some challenges processing that DNA. He suggested that it may be a mixture from several people, not just two people, which we know from experts can make it really hard to extract an individual profile from. He says they have put some of the DNA they found into Cotus, the FBI's national datab bank. No hits yet. But despite all this, he says he still does believe that the unknown DNA may be the suspects and that he still very much sees DNA as a viable lead for them, but that it's just going to take some time. Craig. >> All right, Liz Quitz. Liz, thank you. And as always, if you have any information that could be helpful in this case, there's the phone number right there. Call the FBI's tip line, 1 800 call FBI. >> All right. >> All right, guys. Up next, if you've ever been priced out of seeing your favorite artist in concert, there's a new trial underway that could have a major impact on the cost of those tickets. Our own Savannah Sers has more details. Hey, Savannah. >> Hey there. Good morning. We've all felt that frustration of trying to get our hands on concert tickets, right? Well, could this new trial help that process and those prices? That's coming up on Today. >> Back now with In-Depth today and a trial underway that could shake up the concert ticket industry. >> Yes. If you've ever lamented the cost of a concert or all those added fees, this trial could impact you. >> The Department of Justice is accusing Live Nation of monopolizing the ticket industry. NBC's Savannah Sers is following this case closely. Savannah, good morning. >> Hey there. Good morning, you guys. So more than a decade ago, Live Nation merged with popular ticket seller Ticket Master. But a complaint filed almost two years ago by the DOJ argues that together they now control roughly 80% or more of primary ticketing for concerts, which has allowed them to tack on those fees and increase prices for fans. So if the Justice Department wins their case, what does this ultimately mean for consumers? Well, potentially prices for fans to sing about. Why is one ticket starting to cost $1,000 before tax? >> It's a sentiment concert goers know all too well. >> I'm at the point where concerts are not seeing me until these prices get lower. >> Ticket prices are soaring and so is frustration. But a new trial underway could transform the ticketing industry with the Department of Justice arguing that Live Nation, the owner of Ticketmaster, operates a monopoly in direct violation of antitrust laws. At issue, the company's control of venues, promotion, and ticketing. Last year, Live Nation hosted close to 55,000 events globally and sold more than 646 million tickets and is set to host some of this year's hottest acts, including Harry Styles >> and Lady Gaga. But some fans are already finding it difficult to obtain and afford tickets. >> Oh my god. Tickets to see Lady Gaga in New York is a total bloodbath for the Mayhem ball. >> A struggle many will remember from ticket master's mishandling of the 2022 Taylor Swift era tour ticket sale. They're going for resale on stuff for like $8,000 with fans upset by price tags in the thousands and a difficult process that shut many out. At the time, Swift herself describing what unfolded as excruciating and more artists are coming forward to advocate for change. Kid Rock, a potential witness in this trial, has been a vocal critic. >> I believe music fans and artists have been getting screwed for far too long by the ticketing system. Now, the merger between Live Nation and Ticketmaster lies in a jury's hands. >> I think the government is going to probably try to pursue a breakup either between the Live Nation arm and Ticketmaster or within Live Nation. >> What could this ultimately mean for the consumer, for the concert goer? >> The hope would be that you would have more options, lower prices, a better ticketing experience. >> So, on that note, if the jury decides to, you know, side with the ticket buyers, when can we actually see some changes? So, it would probably take a while for there to be any real change for the consumer in the ticketing landscape. But that being said, there is a chance that they could be ordered to pay damages to customers in different states, which could obviously be a big deal for consumers in addition to a potential of lower ticket prices if there is more competition in the market. For its part, though, I will say Live Nation does say that 75% of all US concert tickets are already priced below $100. and they really put the blame on those scalpers who do the ticket pricing gouging there for consumers. That's what they say. This could last though up to six weeks unless they settle. So, a lot to watch for. >> That was a good point there. >> Secondary market as they call it. >> Yes, exactly. Thank you. >> Thank you, >> Mr. Ocher. What are we looking at, good sir? >> Well, we are looking at the blood moon. This is a full moon, the worm blood moon. Uh, and it appears red. This is a total lunar eclipse of the moon as we pass between it and the sun. And there's something that's called RA scattering. It filters out our Earth's atmosphere, filters out the blue light rays, and so just concentrates the red. And so that's why it looks like this reddish blood moon. Really kind of cool. Now, as we take a look at what's going on right now, we've got tomorrow's these showers and thunderstorms racing across the Midwest. Large hail, damaging wind, gust, wind gust possible. In fact, we've got a risk of severe weather right now stretching from Dallas all the way up into Missouri. Wind gusts of up to 60 mph. Can't rule out a tornado or two. This continues on on Thursday with rain overrunning the Great Lakes into the Northeast. Again, the severe risk for Thursday in parts of Oklahoma and Texas, stretching from San Angelo, Amarillo, just to the west of Oklahoma City. We're looking for some fairly heavy rain stretching from Fort Worth and Oklahoma City all the way into Pittsburgh and parts of the Northeast as well. That's what's going on around the country. Here's what's happening in your neck of the woods. That's your latest weather. #PSSP. >> All right, we got a good one for you guys. Give a little sneak peek of pop star. The numbers are in and the ladies of country music are absolutely dominating Billboard's popular music charts. There's two of them. Let's start with Ella Langley and her hit that's sitting on top of the hot >> 100 way to leave. Drinking Jack all by myself. He's choosing Texas. I can tell. >> I like it. >> That is choosing Texas. That's a country song that's jumped above pop stars like other songs like Olivia Dean, Alex Warren, even Taylor Swift to take the number one spot. She's not the only country female artist up there. Let's take a listen to this hit. This is helping the Georgia native Megan Moroni secure her own career milestone on Billboard's albums chart. Wish I didn't get just let you take me out. Break your bed. Make it all I'm ta
